title: Dealing with spray foam insulation in a loft
subtitle: What to do when spray foam has been incorrectly applied and is causing mortgage or sale problems
excerpt: Spray foam insulation was widely installed in UK lofts between 2010 and 2020. Many mortgage lenders now reject properties where it is present because it bonds to the roof structure and masks defects. This is the remediation decision guide: when removal is necessary, what it costs, who does it, and how to re-insulate after.
sourceNotes: RICS guidance on spray foam insulation in residential property (2021). Royal Institution of Chartered Surveyors UK residential mortgage valuers' guidance. Energy Saving Trust 'Spray foam insulation' advisory note. Which? 'Spray foam insulation: what you need to know' (2023).

glossaryTerms.definition: Polyurethane foam applied as a liquid that expands and sets to form a rigid or semi-rigid insulation layer. Two types are used in lofts: open-cell (softer, lower density) and closed-cell (rigid, higher density, higher performance). Both can bond to timbers and tiles, complicating roof inspection and repair.
A lower-density spray foam where the cells remain open after setting. Softer and more flexible than closed-cell foam, and easier to remove. Typical R-value is lower per millimetre than closed-cell.

A higher-density spray foam where the cells seal after setting, creating a rigid, waterproof material. Higher insulation performance per millimetre but significantly harder to remove without damaging the roof structure.

content.text: Spray foam became popular in UK lofts in the 2010s, often installed by companies operating under ECO1 and Green Deal funding schemes. The insulation works: it seals gaps and adds thermal resistance. The problem is structural:

bonds to rafters, tiles, and sarking felt. A surveyor cannot inspect the roof structure without cutting away the foam. Moisture trapped behind the foam accelerates timber decay without any visible warning. As a result, the RICS advises valuers that spray foam present in a loft should trigger additional investigation, and most high-street mortgage lenders now either decline or heavily discount properties with spray foam present.
The first question: which type of foam?
looks spongy, yellowish-white, and you can compress it with a finger.

is rigid, typically yellow or cream, and sounds hollow when tapped. Open-cell foam is generally easier and cheaper to remove. Closed-cell foam is harder: it bonds to the roof tiles themselves and removing it risks cracking or lifting slates.
Do you actually need to remove it?

If you are not selling or remortgaging, and the roof timbers are sound, removal is not urgent. Spray foam in place for years without signs of decay poses no active danger. Get a structural engineer to inspect the timbers before deciding.

If you are selling or the lender requires removal, that is the only path. Encapsulation coatings are marketed as an alternative but most mortgage lenders do not accept them. Confirm with your specific lender before considering this route.
Costs and who does the work

Spray foam removal is specialist work. Costs for a typical three-bedroom semi with open-cell foam across a whole roof void run from 1,500 to 3,000 pounds. Closed-cell foam on rafters where the tiles must be temporarily lifted costs 3,000 to 6,000 pounds or more. The variation depends on access, foam thickness, and whether any timbers need replacing after removal.

Use a contractor registered with RICS spray foam removal guidance or with TrustMark. Ask for a structural survey of the timbers as part of the contract. You need written confirmation that the roof structure is sound to satisfy a mortgage valuer.

After removal and timber inspection, re-insulate at ceiling level with 270 mm of mineral wool in two layers (100 mm between joists, 170 mm across joists at 90 degrees). This is the standard cold-loft top-up. Do not re-insulate between rafters using spray foam: use rigid PIR board or mineral wool batts fitted with a clear 25 mm ventilation gap between the top of the insulation and the sarking felt.
If the original installer is still trading

Many lofts were foam-insulated under ECO1 or Green Deal schemes with guarantees. Contact the original installer or scheme operator first. The National Insulation Association has a consumer protection scheme and some cases have been resolved under those warranties at no cost to the homeowner.
